keyMyths.title: The Oracle of Delphi and Oedipus
keyMyths.description: The Oracle of Delphi prophesizes that Oedipus will kill his father and marry his mother. Despite attempts to avoid this fate, Oedipus unknowingly fulfills the prophecy, highlighting the power and inevitability of oracular pronouncements.

keyMyths.title: Cassandra's Prophecy of Troy's Fall
keyMyths.description: Cassandra, gifted with prophecy by Apollo, is cursed to never be believed. She foresees the destruction of Troy by the Trojan Horse, but her warnings are ignored, leading to the city's demise and illustrating the tragedy of unheeded visions.

keyMyths.title: The Sibylline Oracles and Roman Destiny
keyMyths.description: The Sibylline Oracles, a collection of prophetic verses, were consulted by Roman leaders during times of crisis. These oracles were believed to reveal the destiny of Rome and provided guidance on religious practices and state affairs, showing the influence of prophecy on Roman politics and religion.
keyMyths.source: Various Roman historians and poets, referencing the *Sibylline Books*

keyMyths.title: The Vision of Er in Plato's Republic
keyMyths.description: Er, a soldier who dies and returns to life, recounts his vision of the afterlife, including the judgment of souls and the workings of the cosmos. This vision provides a philosophical framework for understanding morality, justice, and the consequences of one's actions.

keyMyths.title: The Norse Völva's Prophecy in Völuspá
keyMyths.description: In the *Völuspá*, a völva (seeress) recounts the history of the world, from its creation to its eventual destruction in Ragnarök, and its subsequent rebirth. Her prophecy reveals the cyclical nature of Norse cosmology and the inevitability of fate, even for the gods.

sources.name: Fontenrose, Joseph. *The Delphic Oracle: Its Responses and Operations with a Catalogue of Responses.*
sources.description: A comprehensive study of the Delphic Oracle, examining its historical context, operational procedures, and the nature of its responses.
sources.title: Fontenrose, Joseph. *The Delphic Oracle: Its Responses and Operations with a Catalogue of Responses.*

sources.name: Parke, H. W. *Greek Oracles.*
sources.description: A detailed overview of various Greek oracles, their role in society, and the methods of divination employed.
sources.title: Parke, H. W. *Greek Oracles.*

sources.name: Curry, Patrick. *Prophecy and Power: Astrology in Early Modern England.*
sources.description: Explores the historical and social impact of prophecy and astrological beliefs in shaping political and cultural landscapes.
sources.title: Curry, Patrick. *Prophecy and Power: Astrology in Early Modern England.*

sources.name: Davidson, Hilda Ellis. *Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions.*
sources.description: Examines the role of prophecy and seeresses (völur) in Norse paganism, providing insights into their social and religious significance.
sources.title: Davidson, Hilda Ellis. *Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions.*

sources.name: Plato, *Timaeus*
sources.description: Plato's dialogue explores the nature of the cosmos and includes philosophical musings on the nature of vision and understanding.
sources.title: Plato, *Timaeus*
